title: Cat Passing Blood Clots in Urine: Triage Guide for Pet Parents
meta_desc: Find out what blood clots in cat urine mean, the urgency level for a vet visit, and how photos help your vet diagnose the issue quickly.
content_html:
TL;DR: Blood clots in cat urine indicate significant inflammation or injury; seek a veterinary appointment within 24 hours, or immediate emergency care if your cat is straining or unable to urinate.

What does it mean if my cat is passing blood clots in their urine?

When a cat passes distinct blood clots, it indicates that there is enough bleeding in the urinary tract for the blood to aggregate before being expelled. This is often caused by severe inflammation, bladder stones, or Feline Lower Urinary Tract Disease (FLUTD). Unlike pink-tinged urine, clots suggest a more concentrated site of irritation or injury within the bladder or urethra.

When is blood in my cat's urine a veterinary emergency?

  • Urgency Level: Medium. While seeing blood is distressing, it is generally not an immediate life-threatening emergency unless it is accompanied by an inability to urinate.
  • Critical Emergency: If your cat is straining, vocalizing in the litter box, or producing no urine at all, this is a critical blockage and requires immediate ER care.
  • Routine Urgent Care: If your cat is otherwise acting normally, you should still seek a veterinary appointment within 24 hours to manage pain and prevent progression.

How does taking a photo of my cat's urine help the triage process?

  • Visual Evidence: Taking a photo of the urine and clots provides your veterinarian with vital diagnostic data regarding the severity of the bleeding and size of the clots.
  • Diagnostic Accuracy: Photos help the vet determine whether the substance appears to be pure blood or mixed with mucus or crystals.
  • Case Prioritization: Visual data helps the clinic prioritize your cat's case and choose the appropriate diagnostic tests more efficiently.

Clinical Context (Merck Veterinary Manual)

Hematuria (blood in the urine) can be a sign of feline lower urinary tract disease (FLUTD) and may indicate underlying conditions such as urinary tract infection, neoplasia, trauma, urethral plugs, urolithiasis (urinary stones), or sterile cystitis. Urolithiasis, a common finding in cats, can manifest with clinical signs including hematuria and dysuria (straining to urinate). Matrix-crystalline plugs, often found in the urethra, are a common cause of urethral obstruction. If urethral obstruction occurs, the cat may frequently attempt to urinate, producing only small amounts or nothing, and may exhibit pain. Complete obstruction can lead to uremia within 36-48 hours, resulting in depression, anorexia, vomiting, diarrhea, dehydration, coma, and death within approximately 72 hours, making it an emergency requiring immediate veterinary attention.

Chapter: Urology

Source: The Merck Veterinary Manual, 11th Edition (Page 1531)

faqs.question: Could my cat have a urinary tract infection (UTI)?
faqs.answer: While UTIs are common in humans, they are actually less common in young cats. Blood in a cat's urine is more frequently caused by sterile inflammation (cystitis) or bladder stones.

faqs.question: Is blood in cat urine painful?
faqs.answer: Yes, hematuria is usually associated with inflammation of the bladder wall, which can cause significant discomfort, cramping, and a frequent urge to urinate.

faqs.question: What should I do if my male cat is straining with blood clots?
faqs.answer: Male cats are at high risk for life-threatening urinary obstructions. If a male cat is straining and passing clots, treat it as a medical emergency and go to a vet immediately.
